text: in Dune the bene gesserit tells paul "An animal will chew off its own leg to get out of a trap. what will you do?" the gom jabbar is supposed to  test your ability to withstand short term discomfort in the knowledge you will be better off in the long term. I always found it interesting Herbert wrote this as an effective test for "can this person be trusted to do great things and not mess it up" the stakes are obvious much less epic but prioritizing comfort and will let decades of your one singular precious life fly by while nothing changes but your stack of minor resentments.
response_to_thread: too many smart people unable to optimize anything but their local list of tasks they were just given, never having an honest thought about their own trajectory unless forced to, and then always choose the path of least negative emotion

10s of years of life wasted at a company you never questioned, millions of dollars lost because you felt slight negative emotion over making sure you are priced correctly, endless skills never learned for the comfort of never being opinionated
it's absurd how common it is to feel like you'll make zero progress in your career if you continue doing what you do now for another year

text: i think it's interesting that they chose to build their own browser instead of providing an extension or some agent that works with all browsers. getting people to change platforms has never been  a particularly achievable task
response_to_thread:
I tested OpenAI Atlas today. my mind is blown!

This is the most mid AI product of the year and I stopped using it after 10 minutes. 

> it offers 0 benefits if you have a chatgpt plus,
> it's chromium, so not even revolutionary,
> i've seen better ai browsers

let me explain:

I may be a bit too stiff about this, but i don't buy much into the concept of AI browsers. yes they are a good utility to have BESIDES your real browser, but not totally replace it. 

and guess what i won't do for a company that trains on proprietary data and is the most data-hungry entity of the 2025 tech: let them know more about me on the web.

obviously this is setting the table for ad agencies to bombard you once they know enough about you. I personally use Google Chrome, and i know they're no saints, but they've been munching off my data for years now, might as well continue with the devil i know than the devil i don't. for some reason, i trust Google more than OpenAI, and that's not because i trust Google at all! 

and the utilities these browsers offer are not even interesting. Transcribing YouTube videos, or summarizing a page, or planning a trip to Mogadishu doesn't cut it for me. it's honestly underwhelming.

so if you think this is a "rival" to Chrome, you clearly need to step off the X bubble and meet the real world, where so many people still don't know what ChatGPT is, or what their current browser is called. 

Yes, the future of web browsing will be different, and the ground is pretty shaky for who will rule the WW2 of browsers in the next 3-10 years, but these mfs are not breaking a sweat to convince the users to switch from their lifelong browsers to a data-thirsty AI company that is sooo gonna use that data.

text: these days i spend my time mostly just cycling between the two. either exhausted from working or stressed im not. took two hours off today to clean my room and read a book. i think the work ended up going faster bc of fresh eyes
response_to_thread: Exhaustion that comes from inaction is infinitely worse than exhaustion that comes from action. Feel like you have too much on your plate, the alternative is literally letting thoughts fester in your mind until you go insane. Store that reminder in the back pocket. Use it often

text:
some questions: could the correct answer generator (which comes from being unusually insightful or analytical) be a trap in its own? because when you’re overwhelmed or ashamed it tells you to double down on analysis instead of noticing ur feelings

Something I tell people a lot is "you can’t reason someone out of something they didn’t reason their way into.”  

Reading this made me realize for the first time that that also applies to yourself, and sometimes (even often) the solution does not lie through logic at all, which is actually annoying bc the actual heuristic is far less clear and rule based
response_to_thread: I spent ~25 years of my life developing what I might call a “~correct answer generator” in my head. I put it together from doing a lot of reading and thinking and talking to people. This has been at the heart of a lot of what has gone well in my life. But lately I’m reflecting…

text: in my experience negativity is genuinely contagious, optimism is a vital force field against existential despair/ giving up prematurely. if i lend my ears to doomers i start feeling like life is a game i’ve already lost
response_to_thread: It has become more important than ever to only associate with optimistic people online. Don't have to be positive all the time. Just of the belief that things aren't as bad as they seem. Obvious why. Nothing you don't already know but always a good self check. Life depends on it
